title: Who moves up and why? A closer look at the new World Bank Group Country Income Classifications Release
gist: The World Bank's July 2026 Country Income Classifications update moved six of 218 assessed economies into a higher income tier and none down — Jordan, Micronesia, the Philippines, Sri Lanka and Viet Nam to upper-middle income, Togo to lower-middle income — via six distinct paths ranging from genuine export-led growth (Viet Nam) to a pure population-count revision (Togo) and a national-accounts rebasing that added 10% to measured GDP (Jordan).

themes.theme: Six income upgrades, six different drivers

insights.claim: The World Bank's July 2026 Country Income Classifications update moved six of 218 assessed economies into a higher income tier and none downward: Jordan, Micronesia, the Philippines, Sri Lanka, and Viet Nam rose from lower-middle to upper-middle income, and Togo rose from low to lower-middle income.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A rare positive-only reclassification cycle; each upgraded country becomes progressively less eligible for concessional development financing tied to income-group status.
insights.quote: none of the countries assessed moved down

insights.claim: Viet Nam's exports surged more than 15% in both 2024 and 2025 while GDP grew 7% and 8% in those years respectively, and GNI expanded at an average of 10% annually between 2021 and 2025 — one of the strongest sustained runs in the region — driving its move to upper-middle income.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Confirms Viet Nam's export-led model remains one of the fastest-growing income trajectories in Asia, supportive of continued FDI-driven manufacturing theses.
insights.quote: GNI expanded at an average of 10% annually between 2021 and 2025 — one of the strongest sustained runs in the region.
insights.locator: Viet Nam tells a story of growth

insights.claim: The Philippines' reclassification to upper-middle income reflected broad-based expansion: GDP grew at an average of 5.8% per year over five years, with gains across all major industries rather than a single sector.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Broad-based (not single-sector) growth suggests the Philippines upgrade is structurally durable rather than commodity-cycle driven.
insights.quote: GDP grew at an average of 5.8% per year over five years, reflecting gains across all major industries

insights.claim: Sri Lanka's move to upper-middle income came just three years after its 2022 economic collapse, with real GDP growing 5% in 2025 on a rebound in financial and tourism services, but the country only narrowly crossed the income threshold.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A fragile, narrow crossing — Sri Lanka's upper-middle status could be reversible if the tourism/financial rebound stalls.
insights.quote: the country only narrowly crossed the threshold
insights.locator: Sri Lanka is a story of recovery

insights.claim: Jordan's upgrade to upper-middle income was driven mainly by a national-accounts rebasing exercise that found the economy was nearly 10% larger than previously estimated, combined with steady 2.8% growth in 2025 — not primarily by real acceleration in economic activity.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A reminder that income-classification jumps can be statistical artifacts (rebasing) rather than real economic gains — check whether a reclassified country actually grew or was merely re-measured before pricing in a development thesis.
insights.quote: the economy was nearly 10% larger than previously estimated
insights.locator: Jordan was reclassified because of a comprehensive revision

insights.claim: Togo's move from low to lower-middle income was primarily a population-revision effect: the 2022 census cut Togo's population estimate by 11.7%, mechanically raising per-capita GNI even though GDP grew 'only' 5.9% in 2025.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Illustrates a second measurement-artifact channel — denominator (population) revisions can move a country across an income threshold independent of real income growth; a caveat for using income-tier status as a pure macro growth signal.
insights.quote: the country's population estimate was reduced by 11.7%
insights.locator: Togo moved from low to lower-middle income

insights.claim: Micronesia's upgrade to upper-middle income reflected modest, steady growth from a prolonged COVID-19 recovery led by construction and agriculture, tempered by a significant decline in net primary income.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: The weakest of the six upgrades — a decline in net primary income partly offset domestic growth, flagging Micronesia as the most marginal upgrade to watch for reversal.
insights.quote: A significant decline in net primary income tempered the overall gains.
insights.locator: Micronesia achieved modest but steady growth

insights.claim: Income-classification thresholds are pegged to GNI per capita measured via the Atlas methodology (which smooths short-term exchange-rate swings) and are adjusted annually for inflation using the SDR deflator, a weighted average of the GDP deflators of China, Japan, the UK, the US, and the Euro Area, plus USD exchange-rate movements.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Explains why classification shifts can result from currency and deflator mechanics rather than real growth — a necessary caveat when reading World Bank income-tier headlines as pure development signals.
insights.quote: adjusted annually for inflation using the Special Drawing Rights (SDR) deflator — a weighted average of the GDP deflators of China, Japan, the UK, the US, and the Euro Area
insights.locator: To keep income classification thresholds fixed

insights.claim: Since 1987 the share of the world's economies classified as low-income has fallen from 30% to 11%, while the number of economies the World Bank assesses annually has grown from 163 in 1987 to 218 in 2025.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Confirms the multi-decade global convergence trend (fewer low-income countries) continues — a useful base rate for development and emerging-market allocation theses.
insights.quote: the share of economies classified as low-income declining from 30% to 11%

insights.claim: Country income classifications directly determine which countries can access concessional loans and development assistance, so the six countries just upgraded face a glide path toward reduced eligibility for concessional financing.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Direct policy implication: the six upgraded countries will likely need to rely increasingly on market-rate borrowing or FDI as concessional financing eligibility narrows.
insights.quote: the classifications inform which countries can access concessional loans and development assistance
